title: French lentil soup
subtitle: Puy lentils with onion, carrot, thyme, and a splash of red wine vinegar.
excerpt: Puy lentils simmered with onion, carrot, celery, and thyme, then part-blended to give a creamy texture without losing all the whole lentils. A splash of red wine vinegar at the end lifts it. Plain-sounding, quietly excellent.
sourceNotes: Lentil soups appear throughout French regional cooking, particularly in the Auvergne and Burgundy where Puy lentils originate. Puy lentils have held a protected designation of origin (AOP) since 1996, though the name refers to the Le Puy-en-Velay area of Haute-Loire rather than to a standardised recipe. The vinegar finish is characteristic of French potage lentilles and distinguishes this from British-style lentil soups.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. The texture is slightly softer after freezing but still very good.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Makes an excellent batch-cook lunch soup.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for four days. Puy lentils hold their shape better than red lentils on reheating.

content.text: Puy lentils hold their shape when cooked, which gives this soup a different character from the smooth purées made with red lentils. Part-blending with a stick blender for thirty seconds creates a thick, creamy base while leaving whole lentils to give the soup texture. You control the balance.
The splash of red wine vinegar at the end is not optional. It sounds like a small thing and it is, but the soup tastes flat without it. Lentils need acid the way meat needs salt.
A large saucepan and stick blender. No soaking needed for Puy lentils.

Warm {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the onion, carrot, and celery. Season with salt and cook for 10 minutes until softened. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook for 2 minutes more.

Add {{puy-lentils}} of rinsed Puy lentils, {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook uncovered for 25–30 minutes until the lentils are completely tender.

Remove the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Use a stick blender to blend for 20–30 seconds only: this thickens the soup without destroying all the lentils. Stir in {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve with chopped flat-leaf parsley.

items.symptom: Lentils are still firm after 30 minutes
items.cause: Old lentils take longer to cook, as do lentils simmered in a very acidic stock.
items.fix: Add more hot water and continue simmering. Do not add the vinegar until the lentils are fully cooked — acid prevents softening.

items.cause: Too much blending, or stock reduced heavily.
items.fix: Stir in hot water or stock and adjust seasoning.

items.cause: Acid was not added, or lentils absorbed all the flavour from the aromatics.
items.fix: A splash more red wine vinegar lifts the soup immediately. Taste after adding it.

items.cause: Puy lentils do turn brownish when cooked; this is normal. Adding spinach or some fresh parsley blended in brightens the colour.
items.fix: No fix needed if the flavour is good. For colour, stir in a small handful of spinach before blending.

content.text: Potage de lentilles is a workhorse of French home cooking, particularly in the Auvergne where Puy lentils are grown. The lentils are earthy and peppery with a naturally firm texture that holds up to long cooking. The French domestic version is rarely the smooth purée served in restaurants; home cooks part-blend or leave the lentils entirely whole, then add the vinegar finish that distinguishes the French approach from most other European lentil soups.
